title: Excitation Flow, Positivity, and Fisher Information for Open Subsystems of an $N$-Qubit Network
abstract: We derive closed-form propagators for any $K$-qubit subsystem of a closed $N$-qubit network with a single conserved excitation. A single transition amplitude simultaneously controls excitation flow between subsystems, the positivity and complete positivity of every propagator, the entanglement entropy of every subsystem, and the quantum Fisher information for global parameters. Positivity and complete positivity coincide, determined solely by the direction of excitation flow, independently of subsystem size, coherence, or entanglement structure. A propagator is positive and completely positive if and only if it contracts the subsystem state toward its fixed point. The ensemble of propagators collectively constrains global properties inaccessible to any single subsystem. For single-qubit subsystems, we characterize the ensemble's fixed-point distribution and domain of positivity, finding a band of states that lies inside the positivity domain of every propagator yet is never visited by the physical dynamics. The quantum Fisher information decomposes into state and process contributions over any observation window $[t_1,t_2]$, with the state contribution bounded while the process contribution grows secularly. The total Fisher information is minimal when all future propagators are nonpositive and not completely positive, and near its maximum when they are positive and completely positive.
summary: This paper develops an analytical framework to study the dynamics of subsystems within a larger N-qubit network. It reveals that the direction of excitation flow uniquely determines the positivity and complete positivity of the dynamical maps and links these properties to the sensitivity of the system for parameter estimation via Quantum Fisher Information.
main_problem: Investigating what an observer can infer about a closed N-qubit network by observing its correlated open subsystems, specifically addressing non-Markovian and non-completely positive dynamics.
main_result: The authors show that a single transition amplitude controls excitation flow, positivity, complete positivity, entanglement entropy, and quantum Fisher information; they also find that positivity and complete positivity coincide and are determined solely by the direction of excitation flow.
method: Analytical derivation of closed-form propagators using an operator-sum representation and a decomposition of Quantum Fisher Information into state, process, and cross contributions.
model_or_system: A closed N-qubit network with a single conserved excitation governed by a homogeneous all-to-all XXZ Hamiltonian.
key_observables: Excitation flow, positivity and complete positivity (CP), entanglement entropy, and Quantum Fisher Information (QFI) for global parameters J and N.
important_parameters: Total number of qubits N, subsystem size K, coupling strength J, and the transition amplitude u_d(t).
main_assumptions: The system is restricted to the single-excitation sector (charge q=1) and the global dynamics are unitary.
figures_summary: Figure 4 illustrates the action of dynamical maps on the Bloch ball and trajectories of physical states; Figure 5 shows the domain of positivity for different time intervals; Figure 9 demonstrates the decomposition of Fisher information into state, process, and cross contributions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of subsystem observation, defines the N-qubit XXZ model, derives closed-form propagators, analyzes the conditions for positivity and CP, characterizes entanglement and excitation flow, and concludes with a detailed decomposition and analysis of Quantum Fisher Information.
why_interesting: It provides a unified framework for understanding how local observations of non-Markovian dynamics (including non-CP maps) can be used to infer global properties like coupling strength and network size.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1: An eight-qubit network with all-to-all interactions. Colored circles denote qubits. Shaded regions indicate possible Ki-qubit subsystems described by a density operator ˆρSi(Ki).

FIG. 2: Dynamics along the orbit of a global pure product state with a single excitation localized on one qubit (|1⟩, red), with all other qubits in the ground state (|0⟩, blue). Shading and superscripts distinguish two subsystem classes with distinct reduced dynamics. ˆρ1(K) is a K-qubit subsystem that contains the excited qubit, while ˆρ0(K) excludes it.

FIG. 3: Excitation-flow amplitude φi τ(t, t + ∆t; K) [Eqs. (18), (27)] for ∆t = 0.05, K ∈{1, 2, 3, 4}, and N = 5, plotted against t in units of 2π/(NJ). Positive values indicate net dispersal away from the excited qubit (CP dynamics). Negative values indicate backflow (non-CP).

FIG. 4: Action of the single-qubit dynamical map Λi(0, t) on the z-component of the Bloch ball for N = 5, with bz(0) ∈{−1, −2/3, −1/3, 0, 1/3, 2/3, 1}. Propagators Λi(t1, t2) contract toward the fixed point when φi τ > 0 and expand away from it when φi τ < 0.

FIG. 5: z-component of the domain of positivity (shaded region) of Λi(t, t + ∆t) with N = 5. Contracting propagators (moving toward the fixed point) preserve positivity across the full Bloch interval pi z ∈[−1, 1], whereas expanding propagators (moving away from the fixed point) preserve positivity only on a restricted subset. Overlapping shades appear brown.
